_note: Original Practice questions derived from CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

choices: It raises an IndexError
s
It prints nothing
python.explanation: `len(word)` is 5, so this asks for `word[5]`. The valid indexes are 0 through 4, so there is no character at 5 and Python raises an IndexError. Picking "s" is the tempting answer, because `s` really is the last character. It just lives at index 4, not 5. To get the last character safely, write `word[len(word) - 1]`, or use the shortcut on the next screen.

choices: It throws a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
It prints s
It prints 5
It fails to compile
java.explanation: `word.length()` is 5, so this asks for `word.charAt(5)`. The legal indexes stop at 4, so nothing lives at 5 and the program halts on that line with a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException. "It prints s" is the answer that pulls at you, and for a fair reason: the letter s does sit at the end of the word. Its index is 4, though, not 5, because the count opened at 0. Reach it with `word.charAt(word.length() - 1)`. The code does compile, since the compiler has no way to know what that number will be before the program runs.

choices: "Nav"
"Navi"
"avi"
"vig"
javascript.explanation: Slice includes positions zero through two and excludes position three.

cpp.explanation: The string contains three characters, so its size is three.

python.code:
s = "  hello world  "
parts = s.strip().split()
print(len(parts), parts[-1].upper())
choices: 2 WORLD
2 world
16 WORLD
3 WORLD
python.explanation: Take it one step at a time. `.strip()` trims the spaces on both ends, leaving `"hello world"`. `.split()` then breaks that at the space and gives you the list `['hello', 'world']`, which has 2 items, so `len(parts)` is 2. `parts[-1]` is the last item, `'world'`, and `.upper()` turns it into `'WORLD'`. If you picked 16, you measured the length of the string instead of the list: `len()` counts characters in a string but items in a list.

choices: false then true
true then true
false then false
true then false
java.explanation: `b` is built at runtime by joining `a` with "202", so it is a brand new object that happens to hold the same characters as the "cs202" written in the code. `==` compares the two objects, sees that they are not the same one, and prints false. `.equals()` compares the characters, finds them identical, and prints true. If you picked "true then true", you read `==` as a question about the text. It is a question about the object, and that gap is exactly where the bug lives.

choices: Text you can measure, cut apart, and reshape.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
python.explanation: A string is text in quotes: `"Ada"` or `'Ada'`. `len(s)` tells you how many characters it holds. You reach a single character with `s[i]`, and counting starts at 0, so the last character of a string of length 5 is `s[4]`. `s[-1]` is a shortcut for that last one. `s[1:4]` is a slice: it takes characters 1, 2, and 3, stopping before 4. A string cannot be changed in place, so build a new one instead.

choices: Text is an object in Java, and comparing two of them has one right way.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
java.explanation: A `String` holds text in double quotes: `"Ada"`. It is an object, not a primitive, so it carries methods you call with a dot: `s.length()`, `s.charAt(0)`, `s.substring(1, 4)`, `s.toUpperCase()`, `s.trim()`, `s.split(",")`. Indexes start at 0, so the last character is at `s.length() - 1`. Strings are immutable, so every method returns a new String and changes nothing. Compare two Strings with `a.equals(b)`. Using `==` compares whether they are the same object in memory, which is not the question you meant to ask.

choices: Read and transform text without changing the original value.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
javascript.explanation: Strings are immutable sequences with zero-based indexes. `.length` counts characters, and template literals insert expressions with `${...}`.

choices: Create, index, compare, and combine text.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
cpp.explanation: `std::string` stores text, `.size()` reports its character count, and indexing starts at zero. Strings support comparison and concatenation with compatible operands.

python.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
s = "cat"
s = "b" + s[1:]
print(s)
# bat

s = "cat"
s[0] = "b"
# strings do not
# support item
# assignment
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
python.explanation: Assigning into a string raises a TypeError. Nothing you do to a string edits it in place, so the fix is always the same: build the string you want and put it back in the name.

java.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
java.explanation: `==` asks whether two names point at the same object in memory. `.equals()` asks whether two strings contain the same characters. When you compare text, the second question is the one you meant. The left version compiles cleanly, runs without complaint, and quietly prints the wrong thing.

javascript.explanation: Keep the transformed string returned by the method.

python.prompt: Which statement best explains 'You cannot add a number to a string'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

`"Score: " + 10` raises a TypeError, because plus means one thing for text and something else for numbers, and Python refuses to guess which you meant. Two ways out. Convert the number with `str(10)`, or use an f-string, which is easier to read and is what most people reach for.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
python.explanation: `"Score: " + 10` raises a TypeError, because plus means one thing for text and something else for numbers, and Python refuses to guess which you meant. Two ways out. Convert the number with `str(10)`, or use an f-string, which is easier to read and is what most people reach for.

java.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Java catches type errors before it runs anything'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

Java checks the types of everything while it compiles, which is before a single line of your program has run. So `int n = "hello";` never gets the chance to misbehave. The compiler refuses it outright and prints `incompatible types: String cannot be converted to int`. That is a compile error, and no output appears at all. Compare that with an error thrown while the program is running, which happens after some of your output has already printed. Where the error shows up tells you which kind you are looking at.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
java.explanation: Java checks the types of everything while it compiles, which is before a single line of your program has run. So `int n = "hello";` never gets the chance to misbehave. The compiler refuses it outright and prints `incompatible types: String cannot be converted to int`. That is a compile error, and no output appears at all. Compare that with an error thrown while the program is running, which happens after some of your output has already printed. Where the error shows up tells you which kind you are looking at.

python.prompt: Which statement best explains 'IndexError: string index out of range'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.

`"Bears"` has five characters, so the valid indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Asking for `word[5]` asks for a sixth character that was never there, and Python stops the program rather than invent one. When you see this error, count the characters, then subtract one. That is the highest index you are allowed.
Put every step into one long statement.
python.explanation: `"Bears"` has five characters, so the valid indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Asking for `word[5]` asks for a sixth character that was never there, and Python stops the program rather than invent one. When you see this error, count the characters, then subtract one. That is the highest index you are allowed.

choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.

"Bears" has five characters, so the legal indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. There is no index 5. Ask for `word.charAt(5)` and the program stops with `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 5`. Read that message closely, because it hands you both numbers you need. When it appears, count the characters and subtract one. That is the largest index you are allowed to ask for.
Put every step into one long statement.
java.explanation: "Bears" has five characters, so the legal indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. There is no index 5. Ask for `word.charAt(5)` and the program stops with `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 5`. Read that message closely, because it hands you both numbers you need. When it appears, count the characters and subtract one. That is the largest index you are allowed to ask for.

python.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Catch the result or lose it'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.

`name.upper()` on its own does nothing you can see. It builds a new uppercase string and then throws it away, because you never said where to put it. Write `name = name.upper()` if you want to keep it. Every string method works this way, so it is one habit that covers all of them.
python.explanation: `name.upper()` on its own does nothing you can see. It builds a new uppercase string and then throws it away, because you never said where to put it. Write `name = name.upper()` if you want to keep it. Every string method works this way, so it is one habit that covers all of them.

java.prompt: Which statement best explains 'The `==` bug is quiet, and sometimes it looks like it works'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.

This is the bug that costs Java beginners the most time, and the reason is that `==` sometimes appears to work. Compare two strings you typed directly into your code and Java may reuse one object for both, so `==` returns true and you walk away believing it. Then you compare against a string that came from user input or from a `+`, the object is a fresh one, and the same code silently returns false. Nothing crashes and nothing warns you. The `if` just takes the wrong branch. Use `.equals()` every time you compare text, including the times `==` would have happened to work.
java.explanation: This is the bug that costs Java beginners the most time, and the reason is that `==` sometimes appears to work. Compare two strings you typed directly into your code and Java may reuse one object for both, so `==` returns true and you walk away believing it. Then you compare against a string that came from user input or from a `+`, the object is a fresh one, and the same code silently returns false. Nothing crashes and nothing warns you. The `if` just takes the wrong branch. Use `.equals()` every time you compare text, including the times `==` would have happened to work.

java.prompt: Which statement best explains 'A String method call on its own line is usually a bug'?
choices: `name.trim();` sitting alone as a statement compiles fine and does nothing you can observe. It trims a copy and throws the copy away. The compiler will not warn you, because calling a method and ignoring its answer is legal. If a line calls a String method and does not assign the result, print it, or test it, look at that line again.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

java.explanation: `name.trim();` sitting alone as a statement compiles fine and does nothing you can observe. It trims a copy and throws the copy away. The compiler will not warn you, because calling a method and ignoring its answer is legal. If a line calls a String method and does not assign the result, print it, or test it, look at that line again.
